contract.title: Will the US enter a recession in 2026?
contract.resolution_criteria: Resolves YES if the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) Business Cycle Dating Committee announces that the United States entered an economic recession with a peak month in 2026, on or before 2026-12-31. Resolves NO otherwise.

conditions.label: NBER 2026-peak recession by 2026-12-31
conditions.summary: The NBER Business Cycle Dating Committee publicly announces that the U.S. entered a recession with the peak month occurring in 2026, and this announcement is made on or before 2026-12-31.

contract.narrative: Regulatory developments center on bank capital and leverage tweaks (eSLR, TLAC, CBLR) effective into 2026, which may nudge credit conditions but offer no clear recession signal. Routine TIC flows, Treasury statements, call reports, and state labor data provide only weak, indirect reads. The market awaits 2026 national macro releases and an eventual NBER dating decision to determine whether a 2026 recession occurs.

timeline.title: Treasury International Capital Data for March
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury — Office of Public Affairs
timeline.one_line_why: TIC March data reflect capital flows and are at best an indirect macro/financial signal; alone they don't materially change odds of an NBER 2026 peak.

timeline.title: Economic Policy Statements to TBAC: 2026 - 2nd Quarter​
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury
timeline.one_line_why: Treasury Q2 policy statements provide background macro information (BEA/BLS/CEA inputs) but don't directly indicate an NBER 2026 recession peak.

timeline.title: 2026-04-30 - New Jersey Employment Report: March 2026
timeline.regulator: New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development — Office of Research and Information
timeline.one_line_why: State-level (New Jersey) employment report offers local labor data that minimally informs NBER’s national 2026 recession dating; negligible directional signal.

timeline.one_line_why: Final OCC rule on community bank leverage has limited, indirect effects on lending/stability and provides no clear signal for an NBER‑dated 2026 recession.

timeline.title: Regulatory Capital Rule: Modifications to the Enhanced Supplementary Leverage Ratio Standards for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies and Their Subsidiary Depository Institutions; Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity and Long-Term Debt Requirements for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies 12 CFR Part 324 RIN 3064–AG11 Final rule Effective Date: January 1, 2026

primary_drivers: Final Community Bank Leverage Ratio (CBLR) rule
Regulatory Capital Rule revisions for Category I & II banks
Treasury International Capital data and recent employment reports
heat_panel.explainer: Critical heat driven by major banking rulemaking—final CBLR and broad regulatory capital revisions—amplified by TIC and employment releases; overall momentum eased this week.
